question: How can a Stellar smart account authorize scoped calls, spending limits, time windows, and regulated co-signing without giving every signer full control?

golden.answer: Put the rules in the contract account's __check_auth policy: bind authorization to the invocation tree and enforce allowed contracts/functions, asset and amount caps, expiry windows, and threshold or guardian requirements. Use delegated or session credentials with narrow scope rather than sharing a master key. For many partners, route approvals through policy or verifier contracts instead of adding every participant as an unrestricted signer. Include revocation, pause, replay protection, and auditable policy updates.
keyFacts: Contract-account authorization can bind a credential to specific calls, amounts, assets, and time windows.
Delegated or session credentials should be narrowly scoped instead of exposing a full-control key.
Policy or verifier contracts can express partner co-signing without turning every partner into an unrestricted signer.
avoid: Classic account thresholds are the only way to implement scoped authorization on Stellar.
A session signer must receive the wallet's master private key.

corroboration.claim: Contract-account authorization can bind a credential to specific calls, amounts, assets, and time windows.
Delegated or session credentials should be narrowly scoped instead of exposing a full-control key.
Policy or verifier contracts can express partner co-signing without turning every partner into an unrestricted signer.
Classic account thresholds are the only way to implement scoped authorization on Stellar.
